title: Android Fundamentals
objectives: Understand the Android platform via a real-life analogy
Know the four components + manifest + Gradle/AGP
Distinguish APK vs AAB and what R8 does
Understand ART vs Dalvik vs JVM

sections.markdown:
**Think of a city with a strict building code.**

- **Android** is the CITY. It provides roads (the runtime), water (system services), power (the kernel), and enforces rules (permissions, sandboxes).
- **Your app** is a BUILDING you drop into the city. It has to be constructed per code: standard entry points (the four components), a floor plan the city can inspect (the manifest), and all utility connections labeled (permissions).
- **The AndroidManifest.xml** is your building permit — a document telling the city 'here's who I am, here are my rooms (Activities, Services, Receivers, Providers), here are the utilities I need (permissions), here are the doors that anyone can knock on (intent filters).' The city refuses to let you open (install) without a valid permit.
- **ART (Android Runtime)** is the electrical grid you plug into — it runs your app's code. Old grids (Dalvik) were less efficient; the modern one (ART) pre-compiles hot paths for speed.
- **APK vs AAB** — APK is the whole building, one-size. AAB (App Bundle) is a blueprint the city uses to build a *device-specific* version for each visitor — smaller downloads, faster installs.

**Why this matters:** the sandbox, manifest, and component model are the ideas that make every Android question 'oh THAT makes sense.'

**An Android app** is a set of **four components** (Activity, Service, BroadcastReceiver, ContentProvider) declared in `AndroidManifest.xml`, compiled to DEX bytecode, packaged as an APK/AAB, and executed on the **ART runtime** atop a **customized Linux kernel**. Each app runs in its own process and Linux user sandbox — apps cannot see each other's memory or files except through explicit APIs.
## The four components

rows: Share structured data across apps via `content://`

sections.markdown: ## The manifest — the building permit

sections.code:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android">

  <!-- Utilities we need -->
  <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET" />
  <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.CAMERA" />

  <!-- Hardware/software features (Play uses these to filter incompatible devices) -->
  <uses-feature android:name="android.hardware.camera" android:required="false" />

  <application
      android:name=".MyApp"                    <!-- optional Application subclass -->
      android:label="@string/app_name"
      android:icon="@mipmap/ic_launcher"
      android:theme="@style/AppTheme">

    <!-- Main screen — LAUNCHER category makes it appear in the app drawer -->
    <activity android:name=".MainActivity" android:exported="true">
      <intent-filter>
        <action   android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
        <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
      </intent-filter>
    </activity>

    <!-- A service (foreground music example) -->
    <service android:name=".MusicService"
             android:foregroundServiceType="mediaPlayback"
             android:exported="false" />

    <!-- A boot-completed receiver -->
    <receiver android:name=".BootReceiver" android:exported="true">
      <intent-filter>
        <action android:name="android.intent.action.BOOT_COMPLETED" />
      </intent-filter>
    </receiver>

  </application>
</manifest>

sections.markdown: ## APK vs AAB — the delivery format

rows: The classic ~universal package. One APK = one install file for many devices.
A publishing format. Google Play generates *device-specific* APKs from it (per language, ABI, density).
Only download resources the device needs — often 50%+ smaller installs.

sections.markdown: **The one-liner:** an **APK is the ONE-size box**; an **AAB is the blueprint Play uses to make a smaller box for each device**. Since 2021, Play requires AAB for new apps.
## The compile + run pipeline

sections.code:
Your Kotlin/Java code
         │
         ▼  (Kotlin/javac compile)
JVM .class bytecode
         │
         ▼  (D8: convert to DEX)
.dex bytecode (Dalvik EXecutable)
         │
         ▼  (R8: shrink + obfuscate + optimize + inline)
Optimized .dex + resources → packaged as APK/AAB
         │
         ▼  (Play delivers device-specific APK from AAB)
Device installs → ART runtime executes DEX (AOT + JIT + PGO)

sections.markdown: ## ART vs Dalvik vs JVM

rows: Register-based `.dex`; AOT-compiled on install (early ART); modern ART does JIT + profile-guided AOT recompile

sections.markdown: ## Common beginner mistakes
**Mistake 1: Forgetting `android:exported` on components (Android 12+).** Every activity/service/receiver with an intent filter must set it explicitly, or the app fails to install.

**Mistake 2: Assuming all permissions are install-time.** Since Android 6 (2015), dangerous permissions (camera, location, contacts) are runtime — the user grants each one via a dialog. Always check + request.
**Mistake 3: Uploading a raw APK to Play in 2026.** New apps require AAB since Aug 2021.
**Mistake 4: Disabling R8 to avoid crashes.** The crash comes from missing keep rules; add them (e.g., for reflection, Gson models) rather than disabling shrinking.
**Mistake 5: Targeting too-old `targetSdkVersion`.** Play rejects apps targeting SDK levels more than ~2 years old; also many privacy features require modern targets.
## Best practices
**1. Use AAB and enable R8 + resource shrinking.** Cuts app size 30–50% and speeds install.
**2. Target the latest LTS SDK level within Google's timeline.** Better security, better performance, and Play mandates it.
**3. Set `android:exported` explicitly on every component.** Safer defaults; required on API 31+.
**4. Use `applicationContext` for long-lived references.** Activities die; the app lives while the process does.
**5. Handle runtime permissions with the ActivityResult API.** `registerForActivityResult(ActivityResultContracts.RequestPermission())`.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Ship an AAB.** Build a signed release AAB, upload to Play internal testing, install via Play.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Runtime permission.** Request the camera permission via the ActivityResult API, handle grant/deny/never-again.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Enable R8.** Configure `minifyEnabled true` + `shrinkResources true` + a keep rule for a reflection-based library; compare APK sizes.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Product flavors.** Set up `dev` / `staging` / `prod` flavors with different Firebase configs and base URLs.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What are the four app components?**

**A:** **(1) Activity** — a single screen with a UI (`onCreate` → `onResume` etc.). Entry point for user interaction. **(2) Service** — long-running background component without UI (foreground for user-visible work; bound for IPC; background heavily restricted since Android 8). **(3) BroadcastReceiver** — reacts to system/app broadcast Intents (boot, connectivity, custom events). **(4) ContentProvider** — exposes structured data via `content://` URIs so other apps can query it (Contacts, Media, Calendar — all providers). Each component must be declared in `AndroidManifest.xml`; each has a specific lifecycle managed by the system. Together they form the vocabulary the Android framework uses to talk about your app.

**Q2: What is the AndroidManifest.xml for?**

**A:** The manifest is your app's declaration to the system — everything Android needs to know before running your code. It declares: (1) The **package name** (app identity). (2) Every **component** (Activities, Services, Receivers, Providers). (3) **Permissions** the app requires (`INTERNET`, `CAMERA`, etc.). (4) **Intent filters** — which broadcasts / URLs / share targets each component handles. (5) **Hardware/software features** used (for Play filtering). (6) **Min/target SDK** levels. (7) The **Application subclass** (if any) and app-wide theme/icon/label. The Android build system merges YOUR manifest with library manifests via **Manifest Merger** — a subtle source of bugs when overlapping declarations clash.

**Q3: APK vs AAB — what's the difference?**

**A:** **APK (Android Package)** — the classic installable file. One APK works on many devices, so it packs every language / density / ABI. **AAB (Android App Bundle)** — a publishing format introduced in 2018. You upload one AAB to Play; Play generates device-specific APKs on demand (only the language, screen density, and CPU architecture that device needs). Result: **50% smaller downloads on average**, faster installs, less user data. Since August 2021, new apps on Play MUST be AAB. Under the hood, AAB uses the same DEX bytecode — it's a delivery/optimization improvement, not a new binary format.

**Q4: What is R8?**

**A:** R8 is Android's code shrinker, optimizer, and obfuscator (replaces ProGuard since 2019, on by default in release builds since AGP 3.4). Three jobs: **(1) Shrink** — remove classes and methods never called (dead-code elimination). **(2) Optimize** — inline, constant-fold, simplify code. **(3) Obfuscate** — rename classes/methods to short strings, making reverse-engineering harder and shrinking the DEX. **Resource shrinking** (a separate flag) removes unused drawables/strings/layouts. Together: typical apps drop 30–50% in size. **Keep rules** (`-keep class com.example.Model`) tell R8 not to rename/remove classes accessed via reflection (Gson, Retrofit, etc.) — necessary for those libraries.

**Q5: How does the Android security sandbox work?**

**A:** Each app runs in its **own Linux process** with a **unique Linux user ID (UID)**. The kernel enforces file permissions per-UID, so **app A cannot read app B's files or memory**. Cross-app communication is only possible through Android's explicit APIs: `Intent`s, `ContentProvider`s, `Service` bindings, permissions the user grants. **Permissions** gate access to protected resources (camera, location, contacts, other apps' data). **SELinux** (Security-Enhanced Linux) adds mandatory access control on top: even root can't bypass certain policies. **The Application Sandbox is the foundation of Android security** — apps are 'zoo animals' each in their own enclosure, communicating only through the zoo's official channels.

**Q6: ART vs Dalvik vs JVM?**

**A:** **JVM** — standard Java runtime; stack-based bytecode. Not used on Android. **Dalvik** (Android 1.0 through 4.4) — Google's original Android runtime; register-based `.dex` bytecode designed for constrained mobile hardware; pure JIT. **ART (Android Runtime)** (Android 5.0+) — Dalvik's successor. Also runs `.dex` bytecode but with a much smarter execution strategy: originally AOT-compiled at install time (slow installs, huge apk-size), the modern ART uses a hybrid **JIT + profile-guided AOT**: it JITs at runtime, records which methods are hot, and later AOT-compiles those in the background. **Baseline Profiles** ship a pre-computed profile so hot methods are AOT-compiled from day 1 — significantly reducing first-launch jank.

**Q7: What is the difference between `minSdkVersion`, `targetSdkVersion`, and `compileSdkVersion`?**

**A:** **`minSdkVersion`** — the lowest Android version your app will INSTALL on. Determines your target device coverage; lower = more devices but fewer modern APIs. **`targetSdkVersion`** — the version you've TESTED and OPTED IN to. Android runs your app in a compatibility mode for behaviors that changed after this version. Play REQUIRES targeting a recent SDK (currently within ~2 years of latest). **`compileSdkVersion`** — the SDK the compiler uses. Should generally match the latest stable; lets you use new APIs even if your `min` is older (guard with `Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= ...`). **Rule:** `min` for reach; `target` for correctness on modern devices; `compile` for using new APIs safely.

**Q8: What is Gradle / AGP?**

**A:** **Gradle** — the build system Android uses. Defines the build in `build.gradle(.kts)` files: dependencies, tasks, module structure. **AGP (Android Gradle Plugin)** — the plugin that teaches Gradle how to build Android apps: compile resources, run R8, sign, produce APK/AAB, run tests. Key ideas: **product flavors** (dev/staging/prod variants), **build types** (debug/release), **variants** (flavor × type). AGP versions loosely track Android Studio versions; upgrading needs care because dependency versions and Kotlin/Compose versions must align. AGP 8.x introduced K2 support and non-transitive R class by default. Every serious Android job requires reading and modifying `build.gradle` files fluently.

## Real-world usage

- **Every Play Store app** ships as an AAB with R8 enabled.
- **Product flavors** — enterprise apps commonly have dev/staging/prod flavors with different Firebase configs, base URLs, feature flags.
- **Sandbox + permissions** — the reason 'apps can't see each other's data' just works; also why granting a permission to one app doesn't grant it to another.
- **`Application` subclass** — a canonical initialization point for DI (Hilt uses `@HiltAndroidApp`), logging, crash reporting.
- **Manifest merger** — heavy library use means understanding merged manifests via `Merged Manifest` tab in Studio.
- **Baseline Profiles + Cloud Profiles** — Play generates cloud profiles from real-user telemetry so first-run performance keeps improving.

## Advanced notes

- **Zygote** — a single template process the system forks to create every app process (fast startup, shared classes).
- **SELinux enforce mode** — Android has been SELinux-enforced since Android 4.4; each app's process runs under a specific SELinux domain.
- **Scoped Storage (Android 10+)** — apps can access their own scoped external storage but need SAF for arbitrary user files.
- **Play Feature Delivery** — install-time modules, conditional modules, on-demand modules.
- **APK Signature Scheme v2/v3/v4** — for tamper-proof signing; v3 supports key rotation.
- **App Bundle Explorer / Bundletool** — CLI to inspect an AAB and generate `.apks` archives.
- **KSP (Kotlin Symbol Processing)** — faster than KAPT for annotation processing (Room, Hilt, Moshi codegen).
- **AGP DSL vs Groovy vs Kotlin DSL** — modern projects use `build.gradle.kts` (Kotlin DSL) for type-safe build scripts.

flashcards.back: Declares components, permissions, features, filters, SDK targets.
APK = one-size install file; AAB = publishing format Play uses to generate device-specific APKs (smaller downloads).
Shrink + optimize + obfuscate DEX; typical 30-50% APK reduction with resource shrinking.
Each app runs in its own Linux process/UID; cannot see other apps' files/memory except via Android APIs.
Dalvik = old JIT-only. ART = modern JIT + profile-guided AOT; Baseline Profiles ship precomputed hot-path AOT hints.

options: In the same process as others
The SDK behaviors your app opts in to; Play requires recent one

challenges.text: Read a real manifest and identify: components, permissions, intent filters, exported flags.
Build an AAB and inspect it with bundletool.
Enable R8 + resource shrinking; measure APK before/after.
Add camera runtime permission via ActivityResult API.
Set up product flavors (dev/staging/prod) with different applicationId suffixes.
Add a Baseline Profile and measure startup improvement with Macrobenchmark.
Fix a real R8-related crash by writing the correct keep rule for a reflection-using library.

interviewQuestions: What are the four app components?
APK vs AAB — what's the difference?
How does the Android security sandbox work?
What is the difference between minSdkVersion, targetSdkVersion, and compileSdkVersion?
